cards.text: A reference to const is declared as {{c1::const int& ref = x;}}, which means ref cannot be used to {{c2::modify}} the value it references.

cards.front: Can a reference to const bind to a non-const lvalue?
cards.back: Yes. A const reference can bind to both const and non-const lvalues. It simply means you cannot modify the value through that reference.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
int x = 5;
const int& ref = x;
ref = 10; // error
cards.answer: Cannot modify through a const reference. Even though x is non-const, ref is a const reference so cannot be used to modify x.
cards.explanation: A const reference promises not to modify the referenced value, even if the underlying object is non-const.

cards.text: Unlike non-const lvalue references, const lvalue references {{c1::can}} bind to {{c2::rvalues}}. The rvalue is converted to a temporary object.

cards.question: Why does this code compile?
cards.answer: Const lvalue references can bind to rvalues. The compiler creates a temporary object to hold the value 5, and ref binds to that temporary.
cards.explanation: This is a special exception for const references - they extend the lifetime of temporary objects.

cards.front: What happens to the lifetime of a temporary object bound to a const reference?
cards.back: The lifetime of the temporary is extended to match the lifetime of the const reference. This prevents the temporary from being destroyed immediately.

cards.question: Which lines cause errors?
cards.code:
int x = 5;
int& ref1 = x; // 1
int& ref2 = 5; // 2
const int& ref3 = x; // 3
const int& ref4 = 5; // 4
cards.answer: Line 2 causes an error. Non-const references cannot bind to rvalues.
cards.explanation: Lines 1, 3, and 4 are valid. Line 3 shows const reference to non-const lvalue, line 4 shows const reference to rvalue.

cards.concept: Why const lvalue references are useful
cards.explanation:
Const lvalue references provide important benefits:
  • Can bind to both lvalues and rvalues
  • Prevent accidental modification
  • Enable temporary lifetime extension
  • Commonly used for function parameters
  • Allow passing literals and expressions directly
examples: void print(const int& x);  // Can accept any int expression

cards.text: A const reference can bind to {{c1::lvalues}}, {{c2::non-modifiable lvalues}}, and {{c3::rvalues}}, making it very versatile.
